ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for dried squid fried with fish sauce. FOODON_03302051 (squid food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJM covers squids and cuttlefish as a category. No specific code exists for dried squid fried with fish sauce; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of khô mực chiên mắm is approximately 120 g, representing a shared appetiser or side dish portion as commonly served in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, dried, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152) adapted with frying and fish sauce adjustment; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for dried squid and fried seafood analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for khô mực chiên mắm exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried squid (NDB 15152 / FDC ID 175168) as the primary base, with fat and sodium adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying in oil and marination in fish sauce. Protein and energy values are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA dried squid analogues. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood and protein-dominant fried dish with fish sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for khô mực chiên mắm. GI estimated at 40 based on analogous high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate fried seafood dishes from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is protein-dominant (38 g/100 g) with moderate carbohydrate (12 g/100 g) from fish sauce sugars and light batter; protein and fat content substantially attenuate glycaemic response. GL per 120 g serving = (40 × 12 × 1.20) / 100 = 5.76, rounded to 5.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~980 mg/100 g) from fish sauce and dried squid processing
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid khô mực chiên mắm due to its very high sodium content (~980 mg per 100 g), which can significantly contribute to exceeding the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; a 120 g serving alone provides approximately 1176 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low GI; high protein attenuates glycaemic response but fish sauce adds free sugars
type2_diabetes.guidance: Khô mực chiên mắm has a low estimated glycaemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (12 g/100 g), making glycaemic impact manageable in small portions; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully due to the free sugars contributed by fish sauce and the high sodium load which may exacerbate cardiovascular comorbidities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, elevated cholesterol (~210 mg/100 g), and deep-frying oil contributing saturated and trans fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume khô mực chiên mắm only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high sodium (~980 mg/100 g), elevated dietary cholesterol (~210 mg/100 g), and saturated fat from deep-frying poses a compounded risk for blood pressure elevation and atherogenic lipid profiles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khô mực chiên mắm (dried squid fried with fish sauce) is a Vietnamese seafood dish made by deep-frying or pan-frying rehydrated dried squid and finishing it with a savoury-sweet fish sauce glaze, typically enhanced with garlic, chilli, and sugar. The dish originates from Vietnamese coastal cuisine and is popular as a sharing appetiser or beer snack. In Singapore, it is served at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant. The texture is characteristically chewy and slightly crispy on the exterior, with an intensely umami, salty, and mildly sweet flavour profile from the fish sauce reduction. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~38 g/100 g) due to the concentrated nature of dried squid, and moderate in fat (~12 g/100 g) from the frying process. Sodium content is very high (~980 mg/100 g) owing to both the drying process and fish sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated to be low (~40) due to the dominant protein and fat content attenuating glucose absorption. Clinically, this dish should be consumed in moderation by individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or chronic kidney disease due to its sodium load. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and contains mollusc and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for khô mực chiên mắm exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central dried squid (FDC ID 175168) with adjustments for deep-frying and fish sauce marination. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central dried squid (FDC ID 175168) with upward adjustment of fat_g (+4 g/100 g) and sodium_mg (+400 mg/100 g) to account for deep-frying in oil and fish sauce marination; these adjustments are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA fried seafood analogues. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA dried squid source entry. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA dried squid source entry. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA dried squid source entry. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA dried squid source entry. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA dried squid source entry. 7. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN) international GI tables; no direct measurement exists for this dish. 8. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce may contain non-halal additives depending on brand; halal certification should be verified per restaurant. 9. The cuisine field is set to Vietnamese reflecting the dish's origin, though it is served in Singapore; the multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean-served dishes are applied given the GrabFood Singapore source context. 10. All -1 sentinel values are documented above per g3 requirements.
